sentences.chinese: 毋有復言。」
sentences.literal: Do not speak further."
sentences.idiomatic: Let there be no more memorials on this."

sentences.chinese: 後歲餘，武帝疾，立皇子鉤弋夫人男為太子，拜大將軍霍光、車騎將軍金日磾、御史大夫桑弘羊及丞相千秋，並受遺詔，輔道少主。
sentences.literal: More than a year later Emperor Wu fell ill; he established the son of Lady Gouyi as heir apparent and appointed Grand General Huo Guang, General of Chariots and Cavalry Jin Midi, Imperial Counselor Sang Hongyang, and Chancellor Qianqiu, jointly to receive the testamentary edict and guide the young ruler.
sentences.idiomatic: A year and more afterward the emperor fell mortally ill. He named the Lady of the Hook Clasp's boy crown prince and named Huo Guang as grand general, Jin Midi as general of chariots and cavalry, Sang Hongyang as imperial counselor, and Che Qianqiu as chancellor, charging them together with the deathbed rescript to steer the boy who would succeed him.

sentences.chinese: 武帝崩，昭帝初即位，未任聽政，政事一決大將軍光。
sentences.literal: When Emperor Wu died and Emperor Zhao had just ascended, he did not yet personally hear cases; all government was decided by Grand General Huo Guang alone.
sentences.idiomatic: Emperor Wu died; the young emperor Zhao took the throne but did not yet hold court. Every decision of state passed through Huo Guang alone.

sentences.chinese: 千秋居丞相位，謹厚有重德。
sentences.literal: Qianqiu held the chancellorship, prudent, honest, and of grave virtue.
sentences.idiomatic: Che Qianqiu sat in the chancellery with the same cautious decency that had won the earlier emperors' trust.

sentences.chinese: 每公卿朝會，光謂千秋曰：「始與君侯俱受先帝遺詔，今光治內，君侯治外，宜有以教督，使光毋負天下。」
sentences.literal: Whenever the high ministers assembled at court, Guang said to Qianqiu, "At the outset you and I, my lord marquis, together received the late emperor's testament; now I govern within and you, my lord marquis, govern without—you ought to instruct and oversee me so that I do not betray all under Heaven."
sentences.idiomatic: At each great assembly Huo Guang would remind the chancellor: "We both swore to the late emperor to shepherd the boy. You hold the civil side, I the inner court—counsel me frankly so I do not fail the realm."

sentences.chinese: 千秋曰：「唯將軍留意，即天下幸甚。」
sentences.literal: Qianqiu said, "If only the general will be mindful, then all under Heaven will be greatly fortunate."
sentences.idiomatic: Che Qianqiu answered only, "If the general stays mindful of his charge, the empire is already fortunate."

sentences.chinese: 終不肯有所言。
sentences.literal: In the end he would never add another word.
sentences.idiomatic: He never offered more than that.

sentences.chinese: 光以此重之。
sentences.literal: Guang on this account respected him the more.
sentences.idiomatic: Huo Guang respected him deeply for his reticence.

sentences.chinese: 每有吉祥嘉應，數褒賞丞相。
sentences.literal: Whenever there were auspicious omens, he repeatedly praised and rewarded the chancellor.
sentences.idiomatic: Whenever heaven sent favorable portents, Huo Guang heaped fresh honors on the chancellor.

sentences.chinese: 訖昭帝世，國家少事，百姓稍益充實。
sentences.literal: Through Emperor Zhao's reign the state knew few emergencies, and the people gradually grew richer.
sentences.idiomatic: For the rest of Zhao's short reign the realm stayed quiet and the common folk slowly filled their granaries again.

sentences.chinese: 始元六年，詔郡國舉賢良文學士，問以民所疾苦，於是鹽鐵之議起焉。
sentences.literal: In the sixth year of Shiyuan an edict ordered commanderies and kingdoms to recommend worthy men and literary scholars and asked them what pained the people; thereupon the debate on the salt and iron monopolies began.
sentences.idiomatic: In Shiyuan 6 the throne ordered every commandery to send up worthy literati and asked what ailed the populace—thus began the famous salt-and-iron debate.

sentences.chinese: 千秋為相十二年，薨，謚曰定侯。
sentences.literal: Qianqiu was chancellor twelve years, then died; his posthumous title was Marquis Ding.
sentences.idiomatic: Che Qianqiu died in office after twelve years as chancellor and received the posthumous name Ding, "the Steadfast."

sentences.chinese: 初，千秋年老，上優之，朝見，得乘小車入宮殿中，故因號曰「車丞相」。
sentences.literal: At first, because Qianqiu was old, the emperor favored him: when he came to audience he was permitted to ride a small cart into the palace precincts, and therefore he was nicknamed "Cart Chancellor."
sentences.idiomatic: In his last years the emperor indulged him: he might drive a light carriage right into the palace yards, and courtiers nicknamed him the Cart Chancellor.

sentences.chinese: 子順嗣侯，官至雲中太守，宣帝時以虎牙將軍擊匈奴，坐盜增鹵獲自殺，國除。
sentences.literal: His son Shun inherited the marquisate, rose to Guardian of Yunzhong, and under Emperor Xuandi as general Who Bares Tiger's Teeth campaigned against the Xiongnu; for fraudulently inflating heads taken he committed suicide and the fief was abolished.
sentences.idiomatic: His heir Shun rose to governor of Yunzhong; under Xuandi he led the Tiger-Fang host against the Xiongnu but padded the body count and killed himself when exposed, and the marquisate was struck off.

sentences.chinese: 桑弘羊為御史大夫八年，自以為國家興榷管之利，伐其功，欲為子弟得官，怨望霍光，與上官桀等謀反，遂誅滅。
sentences.literal: Sang Hongyang had been Imperial Counselor eight years; he thought he had brought profit to the state through monopolies and controls, boasted of his merit, wished to obtain offices for sons and younger brothers, nursed resentment against Huo Guang, and with Shangguan Jie and others plotted rebellion; he was then executed and exterminated.
sentences.idiomatic: Sang Hongyang had held the censorate for eight years, convinced he had enriched the treasury with state monopolies. He bragged of it, angled jobs for his kin, resented Huo Guang, and joined Shangguan Jie's cabal—when the plot collapsed his whole line perished.

sentences.chinese: 王訢，濟南人也。
sentences.literal: Wang Xin was a man of Jinan.
sentences.idiomatic: Wang Xin came from Jinan.

sentences.chinese: 以郡縣吏積功，稍遷為被陽令。
sentences.literal: By accumulated merit as a commandery and county clerk he was gradually promoted to magistrate of Beiyang.
sentences.idiomatic: He had begun as a petty clerk and climbed step by step until the court named him magistrate of Beiyang.

sentences.chinese: 武帝末，軍旅數發，郡國盜賊群起，繡衣御史暴勝之使持斧逐捕盜賊，以軍興從事，誅二千石以下。
sentences.literal: Near the end of Emperor Wu's reign campaigns multiplied, bandits sprang up through commanderies and kingdoms, and the embroidered-gown Imperial Counselor Bao Shengzhi, bearing an axe of authority to hunt bandits, acted under military emergency statutes and executed officials down to those below two thousand piculs.
sentences.idiomatic: Late in Wu's reign armies marched without cease and robber bands overran the countryside. The robed censor Bao Shengzhi rode circuit with executioner's axe, invoking wartime powers to behead any local magnate who stood in his way.

sentences.chinese: 勝之過被陽，欲斬訢，訢已解衣伏質，仰言曰：「使君顓殺生之柄，威震郡國，令夏斬一訢，不足以增威，不如時有所寬，以明恩貸，令盡死力。」
sentences.literal: When Shengzhi passed Beiyang he wished to execute Xin; Xin had already stripped for the block and, lifting his face, said, "The commissioner monopolizes the power of life and death and his might shakes every commandery; to behead one Xin in the height of summer would not add to your awe—better to show timely leniency, to make clear your gracious pardon, and let us give our utmost in death."
sentences.idiomatic: When Bao's column reached Beiyang he meant to strike off Wang Xin's head. Xin bared his neck to the chopping block but pleaded: "You hold every man's life in your hand; lopping off one magistrate at midsummer wins you no terror. Spare me once and word will spread that mercy pays—that buys you fiercer loyalty than a pile of heads."

sentences.chinese: 勝之壯其言，貰不誅，因與訢相結厚。
sentences.literal: Shengzhi thought his words heroic, spared him execution, and on that account became closely bound to Xin.
sentences.idiomatic: Bao admired the speech, stayed the blade, and afterward treated Xin as a sworn friend.

sentences.chinese: 勝之使還，薦訢，徵為右輔都尉，守右扶風。
sentences.literal: When Shengzhi's mission returned he recommended Xin, who was summoned as Chief Commandant of the Right Assistant and acting Governor of Youfufeng.
sentences.idiomatic: On returning to the capital Bao recommended him; the court summoned Xin as chief commandant of the western preserves and acting governor of Youfufeng.

sentences.chinese: 上數出幸安定、北地，過扶風，宮館馳道修治，供張辦。
sentences.literal: The emperor often went out to favour Anding and Beidi, passing through Fufeng; the lodges and post roads were repaired and the equipage fully provided.
sentences.idiomatic: Whenever the emperor toured Anding and Beidi his route crossed Youfufeng, and Xin saw that every relay station and lodge stood ready and every curtain and cushion was in place.

sentences.chinese: 武帝嘉之，駐車，拜訢為真，視事十餘年。
sentences.literal: Emperor Wu praised this, halted his carriage, invested Xin in substantive appointment, and he administered affairs for more than ten years.
sentences.idiomatic: Wu was so pleased that he stopped the imperial carriage to confirm Xin in full rank; he governed the commandery for more than a decade.

sentences.chinese: 昭帝時為御史大夫，代車千秋為丞相，封宜春侯。
sentences.literal: Under Emperor Zhao he became Imperial Counselor, succeeded Che Qianqiu as chancellor, and was enfeoffed Marquis of Yichun.
sentences.idiomatic: Under Emperor Zhao he rose to imperial counselor, then replaced Che Qianqiu as chancellor with the marquisate of Yichun.

sentences.chinese: 明年薨，謚曰敬侯。
sentences.literal: The next year he died; his posthumous title was Marquis Jing.
sentences.idiomatic: He died the following year and was canonized as Jing, "the Reverent."

sentences.chinese: 子譚嗣，以列侯與謀廢昌邑王立宣帝，益封三百戶。
sentences.literal: His son Tan succeeded; as a full marquis he joined the plot to depose the King of Changyi and establish Emperor Xuandi, and received an additional enfeoffment of three hundred households.
sentences.idiomatic: His son Tan inherited the fief and, still a full marquis, helped depose the king of Changyi and enthrone Xuandi, earning three hundred extra households.

sentences.chinese: 薨，子咸嗣。
sentences.literal: When he died, his son Xian succeeded.
sentences.idiomatic: Tan was succeeded by his son Xian.

sentences.chinese: 王莽妻即咸女，莽篡位，宜春氏以外戚寵。
sentences.literal: Wang Mang's wife was Xian's daughter; when Mang usurped the throne the Yichun family flourished as maternal kin.
sentences.idiomatic: Wang Mang married Xian's daughter; when Mang seized the throne the Yichun house rode the wave as maternal relatives.

sentences.chinese: 自訢傳國至玄孫，莽敗，乃絕。
sentences.literal: From Xin the fief was transmitted to the great-great-grandson; when Mang fell it was cut off.
sentences.idiomatic: The line ran from Wang Xin down to a fourth-generation heir; it ended when Mang fell.

sentences.chinese: 楊敞，華陰人也。
sentences.literal: Yang Chang was a man of Huayin.
sentences.idiomatic: Yang Chang was a native of Huayin.

sentences.chinese: 給事大將軍莫府，為軍司馬，霍光愛厚之，稍遷至大司農。
sentences.literal: He served in the Grand General's headquarters as a military major; Huo Guang favoured and advanced him, and he was gradually promoted to Grand Minister of Agriculture.
sentences.idiomatic: He clerked in Huo Guang's field headquarters as army major, won the regent's confidence, and climbed to grand minister of agriculture.

sentences.chinese: 元鳳中，稻田使者燕倉知上官桀等反謀，以告敞。
sentences.literal: In the Yuanfeng era the commissioner of paddy fields Yancang learned of Shangguan Jie's rebellious plot and informed Chang.
sentences.idiomatic: During Yuanfeng the paddy-field inspector Yancang uncovered Shangguan Jie's conspiracy and took the news to Yang Chang.

sentences.chinese: 敞素謹累事，不敢言，乃移病臥。
sentences.literal: Chang had always been cautious in successive posts and dared not speak; he then feigned illness and took to his bed.
sentences.idiomatic: Yang was timid by habit and dared not act; he pleaded illness and hid under his covers.

sentences.chinese: 以告諫大夫杜延年，延年以聞。
sentences.literal: He told Remonstrance Grandee Du Yannian, who reported it to the throne.
sentences.idiomatic: He confided instead in remonstrant Du Yannian, who carried the matter to the emperor.

sentences.chinese: 蒼、延年皆封，敞以九卿不輒言，故不得侯。
sentences.literal: Yancang and Yannian were both enfeoffed; Chang, because as one of the nine ministers he had not spoken promptly, therefore did not obtain a marquisate.
sentences.idiomatic: Yancang and Yannian both received fiefs; Yang, though a nine minister, had held his tongue too long and won no marquisate.

sentences.chinese: 後遷御史大夫，代王訢為丞相，封安平侯。
sentences.literal: Later he was promoted to Imperial Counselor, succeeded Wang Xin as chancellor, and was enfeoffed Marquis of Anping.
sentences.idiomatic: He was later promoted to imperial counselor, replaced Wang Xin as chancellor, and enfeoffed as marquis of Anping.

sentences.chinese: 明年，昭帝崩。
sentences.literal: The next year Emperor Zhao died.
sentences.idiomatic: The next year young Emperor Zhao died.

sentences.chinese: 昌邑王征即位，淫亂，大將軍光與車騎將軍張安世謀欲廢王更立。
sentences.literal: The King of Changyi, summoned to the capital, took the throne and behaved wantonly; Grand General Huo Guang and General of Chariots and Cavalry Zhang Anshi plotted to depose the king and enthrone another.
sentences.idiomatic: The prince of Changyi, rushed to the capital, proved a debauchee on the throne. Huo Guang and chariot general Zhang Anshi resolved to pull him down and choose a worthier Liu.

sentences.chinese: 議既定，使大司農田延年報敞。
sentences.literal: Once the plan was settled they sent Grand Minister of Agriculture Tian Yannian to inform Chang.
sentences.idiomatic: When the cabal had fixed its course they sent grand minister of agriculture Tian Yannian to sound Yang Chang.

sentences.chinese: 敞驚懼，不知所言，汗出洽背，徒唯唯而已。
sentences.literal: Chang was terrified, knew not what to say, sweat poured down his back, and he could only nod assent.
sentences.idiomatic: Yang broke into a cold sweat and could only mumble yes.

sentences.chinese: 延年起至更衣，敞夫人遽從東箱謂敞曰：「此國大事，今大將軍議已定，使九卿來報君侯。
sentences.literal: Yannian rose to go to the robing room; Lady Yang suddenly came from the east bay and said to Chang, "This is a great affair of state; now the general's council has decided and they send a nine minister to report to you, my lord marquis.
sentences.idiomatic: Tian stepped out to change robes; Lady Yang slipped from the eastern alcove and hissed at her husband, "This is dynastic life or death. The generals have settled on a coup and a minister of state comes to your door.

sentences.chinese: 君侯不疾應，與大將軍同心，猶與無決，先事誅矣。」
sentences.literal: If you, my lord marquis, do not answer swiftly and show one heart with the general, you still hesitate undecided—and you will be executed before the deed is done."
sentences.idiomatic: Hesitate one heartbeat and you die before dawn—say yes now and mean it."

sentences.chinese: 延年從更衣還，敞、夫人與延年參語許諾，請奉大將軍教令，遂共廢昌邑王，立宣帝。
sentences.literal: When Yannian returned from the robing-room, Chang and his lady conferred with him in whispers, promised to obey the Grand General's orders, and together they deposed the King of Changyi and established Emperor Xuandi.
sentences.idiomatic: When Tian returned, husband and wife whispered their assent with him; Yang then obeyed Huo Guang's orders, and the coalition deposed the king of Changyi and raised Liu Bingyi as Xuandi.

sentences.chinese: 宣帝即位月餘，敞薨，謚曰敬侯。
sentences.literal: A little more than a month after Emperor Xuandi's accession, Chang died; his posthumous title was Marquis Jing.
sentences.idiomatic: Within a month of Xuandi's accession Yang Chang died and received the posthumous name Jing.

sentences.chinese: 子忠嗣，以敞居位定策安宗廟，益封三千五百戶。
sentences.literal: His son Zhong succeeded; because Chang's tenure had fixed the succession and secured the ancestral temples, he received an additional three thousand five hundred households.
sentences.idiomatic: His heir Zhong inherited the title and, because his father had sealed the succession, was granted thirty-five hundred extra households.

sentences.chinese: 忠弟惲，字子幼，以忠任為郎，補常侍騎，惲母，司馬遷女也。
sentences.literal: Zhong's younger brother Yun, courtesy name Ziyou, through Zhong's appointment became a gentleman, was assigned as mounted attendant at court; Yun's mother was Sima Qian's daughter.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhong's younger brother Yun, courtesy Ziyou, entered the palace as a gentleman cadet through his brother's rank and served as mounted attendant; his mother was Sima Qian's daughter.

sentences.chinese: 惲始讀外祖《太史公記》，頗為《春秋》。
sentences.literal: Yun from the first read his maternal grandfather's Records of the Grand Historian and was fairly adept at the Spring and Autumn Annals.
sentences.idiomatic: Yun grew up on his grandfather's Shiji and knew the Spring and Autumn Annals well enough to argue its cases.

sentences.chinese: 以材能稱。
sentences.literal: He was praised for talent and ability.
sentences.idiomatic: The court praised his wit and polish.

sentences.chinese: 好交英俊諸儒，名顯朝廷，擢為左曹。
sentences.literal: He loved to associate with talented scholars of note, became famous at court, and was promoted to Left Assistant.
sentences.idiomatic: He cultivated the capital's brightest scholars, made a name at court, and won appointment as left aide.

sentences.chinese: 霍氏謀反，惲先聞知，因侍中金安上以聞，召見言狀。
sentences.literal: When the Huo clan plotted rebellion, Yun was the first to learn of it; through the Palace Attendant Jin Anshang he reported it and was summoned to describe the situation.
sentences.idiomatic: When the Huo family turned traitor Yun heard first; he tipped off the emperor through palace attendant Jin Anshang and was called in to give chapter and verse.

sentences.chinese: 霍氏伏誅，惲等五人皆封，惲為平通侯，遷中郎將。
sentences.literal: When the Huo clan was executed, Yun and five others were all enfeoffed; Yun became Marquis of Pingtong and was promoted to General of the Gentlemen of the Household.
sentences.idiomatic: After the Huo extermination Yun was one of five ennobled; he received Pingtong county as his fief and was promoted general of the gentlemen of the household.

sentences.chinese: 郎官故事，令郎出錢市財用，給文書，乃得出，名曰「山郎」。
sentences.literal: By custom of the gentlemen cadets each cadet paid out cash to buy supplies and furnish documents before he could go off duty; this was called "mountain gentleman."
sentences.idiomatic: Cadets had long paid cash for stationery and relief before they could leave rotation—office slang called them mountain gentlemen.

sentences.chinese: 移病盡一日，輒償一沐，或至歲余不得沐。
sentences.literal: If one took sick leave for a full day, one forfeited one bath leave; sometimes for more than a year one could not obtain a bath leave.
sentences.idiomatic: A full day's sick leave cost you one of your rare bath-and-rest days; some men went a year without a single leave.

sentences.chinese: 其豪富郎，日出遊戲，或行錢得善部。
sentences.literal: The wealthy cadets played about by day or bribed their way to choice assignments.
sentences.idiomatic: Rich cadets lounged in the markets by daylight or bought cushy billets with silver.

sentences.chinese: 貨賂流行，傳相放效。
sentences.literal: Bribes circulated and were imitated from one to another.
sentences.idiomatic: Graft became habit, each man copying the last.

sentences.chinese: 惲為中郎將，罷山郎，移長度大司農，以給財用。
sentences.literal: As General of the Gentlemen of the Household Yun abolished the "mountain gentleman" system; he shifted the long tally to the Grand Minister of Agriculture to supply funds.
sentences.idiomatic: Yun as chief of the gentlemen abolished the pay-to-leave rule, routed supplies through the grand minister of agriculture, and fed the corps from the public purse.

sentences.chinese: 其疾病休謁洗沐，皆以法令從事。
sentences.literal: Sick leave, rest, petitions, and bath days were all handled according to statute.
sentences.idiomatic: Sick leave, rest days, and audiences were henceforth governed by written regulation.

sentences.chinese: 郎、謁者有罪過，輒奏免，薦舉其高弟有行能者，至郡守、九卿。
sentences.literal: When gentlemen cadets or ushers had offenses they were memorialized for dismissal; he recommended for promotion those of high rank and proven conduct, up to governors and the nine ministers.
sentences.idiomatic: He cashiered erring cadets and ushers by memorial and promoted the ablest, some all the way to governors and nine ministers.

sentences.chinese: 郎官化之，莫不自厲，絕請謁貨賂之端，令行禁止，宮殿之內翕然同聲。
sentences.literal: The cadets were transformed by him; none failed to steel himself, the channel of private requests and bribes was cut off, orders were obeyed and prohibitions enforced, and within the palace there was unanimous accord.
sentences.idiomatic: The corps straightened under him: no more backstairs silver, orders ran clean, and the inner palace spoke with one voice.

sentences.chinese: 由是擢為諸吏光祿勳，親近用事。
sentences.literal: On this account he was promoted Superintendent of the Palace and Privy Treasurer of the Bearer of Gentlemen, became intimate at power's side.
sentences.idiomatic: He was therefore raised to superintendent of the palace and privy treasurer of the gentlemen, standing at the emperor's elbow.

sentences.chinese: 初，惲受父財五百萬，及身封侯，皆以分宗族。
sentences.literal: At first Yun received five million cash from his father; when he himself was enfeoffed marquis he distributed all of it to his kinsmen.
sentences.idiomatic: His father had left him five million cash; the day he became a marquis he gave every coin to his clan.

sentences.chinese: 後母無子，財亦數百萬，死皆子惲，惲盡復分後母昆弟。
sentences.literal: His stepmother had no children; her property also ran to several million; on her death it all passed to Yun, who distributed it again among his stepmother's brothers.
sentences.idiomatic: His childless stepmother owned millions more; when she died it fell to Yun, who shared it with her brothers.

sentences.chinese: 再受訾千餘萬，皆以分施。
sentences.literal: A second time he received assets exceeding ten million, and he distributed all of it in gifts.
sentences.idiomatic: Twice more he inherited sums over ten million and gave them away as fast as they arrived.

sentences.chinese: 其輕財好義如此。
sentences.literal: Thus lightly he treated wealth and loved righteousness.
sentences.idiomatic: That was how lightly he held money and how dearly he held honor.

sentences.chinese: 惲居殿中，廉潔無私，郎官稱公平。
sentences.literal: When Yun was in the palace he was incorrupt and without private favor; the gentlemen cadets praised his fairness.
sentences.idiomatic: Inside the palace he took no bribes and showed no favoritism; the cadets called him just.

sentences.chinese: 然惲伐其行治，又性刻害，好發人陰伏，同位有忤己者，必欲害之，以其能高人。
sentences.literal: Yet Yun boasted of his conduct and governance; his nature was harsh and injurious; he loved to expose others' hidden faults; any colleague who crossed him he was set on harming, using his talent to lord over others.
sentences.idiomatic: Yet he swaggered over his own rectitude, savored cutting others down, ferreted out private scandal, and ruined any peer who crossed him—his cleverness became a bludgeon.

sentences.chinese: 由是多怨於朝廷，與太僕戴長樂相失，卒以是敗。
sentences.literal: Therefore he accumulated many enemies at court; he fell out with Grand Coachman Dai Changle and in the end was ruined on that account.
sentences.idiomatic: He made enemies everywhere, quarreled with grand coachman Dai Changle, and in the end that feud destroyed him.

sentences.chinese: 長樂者，宣帝在民間時與相知，及即位，拔擢親近。
sentences.literal: Changle had known the emperor when he was still among the common people; when Xuandi mounted the throne he pulled him up to stay close.
sentences.idiomatic: Dai had been Xuandi's companion in the lanes before the throne; the new emperor raised him to ride at his side.

sentences.chinese: 長樂嘗使行事肄宗廟，還謂掾史曰：「我親面見受詔，副帝肄，秺侯御。」
sentences.literal: Changle was once sent to act as deputy in the imperial rehearsal at the ancestral temple; returning he said to his clerks, "I personally was received in audience and given edict, assisting the Son of Heaven in the rehearsal while the Marquis of Du held the reins."
sentences.idiomatic: Once, after Dai had served as the emperor's deputy in the temple drill, he bragged to his clerks that he had taken edict face to face while the marquis of Du drove the chariot.

sentences.chinese: 人有上書告長樂非所宜言，事下廷尉。
sentences.literal: Someone memorialized accusing Changle of having spoken beyond what was proper; the matter was sent down to the Commandant of Justice.
sentences.idiomatic: A tipster denounced that boast as lèse-majesté; the case went to the commandant of justice.

sentences.chinese: 長樂疑惲教人告之，亦上書告惲罪。
sentences.literal: Changle suspected Yun had incited the accusation and likewise memorialized accusing Yun of crimes.
sentences.idiomatic: Dai decided Yun had put the accuser up to it and struck back with his own bill of indictment.

sentences.chinese: 高昌侯車奔入北掖門，惲語富平侯張延壽曰：「聞前曾有奔車抵殿門，門關折，馬死，而昭帝崩。
sentences.literal: The Marquis of Gaochang's chariot galloped into the north vee gate; Yun said to Marquis of Fuping Zhang Yanshou, "I have heard that once before a runaway chariot rammed a palace gate, the gate bolt broke, the horse died, and Emperor Zhao perished."
sentences.idiomatic: When the marquis of Gaochang's carriage bolted through the north vee gate, Yun remarked to Zhang Yanshou of Fuping, "They say the last time a team careered into a palace gate, the bar snapped, the horses fell, and Emperor Zhao was dead within days."

sentences.chinese: 今復如此，天時，非人力也。」
sentences.literal: "Now it is so again—this is heaven's timing, not human effort."
sentences.idiomatic: "Here we go again—omens, not accidents."

sentences.chinese: 左馮翊韓延壽有罪下獄，惲上書訟延壽。
sentences.literal: When Han Yanshou, Governor of the Left [Fengyi], was charged and cast into prison, Yun memorialized in his defence.
sentences.idiomatic: When Han Yanshou, governor of left Fengyi, landed in prison, Yun filed a memorial pleading his innocence.

sentences.chinese: 郎中丘常謂惲曰：「聞君侯訟韓馮翊，當得活乎？」
sentences.literal: Gentleman of the Household Qiu Chang said to Yun, "I hear your lordship is pleading for Governor Han—will he get off alive?"
sentences.idiomatic: Cadet Qiu Chang asked whether Yun thought Han would walk out alive.

sentences.chinese: 惲曰：「事何容易！
sentences.literal: Yun said, "How easy do you think the matter is!"
sentences.idiomatic: Yun snapped, "You think that is an easy fix?"

sentences.chinese: 脛脛者未必全也。
sentences.literal: Even the upright are not necessarily spared.
sentences.idiomatic: Even honest men do not always walk free.

sentences.chinese: 我不能自保，真人所謂鼠不容穴銜窶數者也。」
sentences.literal: "I cannot even protect myself—what men call a rat with no hole left gnawing tattered rags."
sentences.idiomatic: "I can barely save my own skin—the proverb about the cornered rat chewing rags fits me too well."

sentences.chinese: 又中書謁者令宣持單于使者語，視諸將軍、中朝二千石。
sentences.literal: Again the Director of Attendants for the Palace Xuan conveyed the Xiongnu envoys' words and displayed them to the generals and the inner court ministers at two thousand piculs.
sentences.idiomatic: Again the palace usher director Xuan relayed the Xiongnu envoys' gossip to the generals and inner-court ministers of two thousand piculs.

sentences.chinese: 惲曰：「冒頓單于得漢美食好物，謂之殠惡，單于不來明甚。」
sentences.literal: Yun said, "The Modun Chanyu found Han's fine foods and treasures stinking and vile; it is abundantly clear the Chanyu will not come south."
sentences.idiomatic: Yun added that Modun had called Han delicacies rotten and foul—proof the Chanyu would never bother to visit.

sentences.chinese: 惲上觀西閣上畫人，指桀、紂畫謂樂昌侯王武曰：「天子過此，一二問其過，可以得師矣。」
sentences.literal: In the upper gallery Yun studied the paintings on the western pavilion, pointed to the portraits of Jie and Zhou and said to Marquis of Lechang Wang Wu, "When the Son of Heaven passes here, if he asks once or twice about their faults, he may gain his teachers."
sentences.idiomatic: Touring the western gallery he pointed at the portraits of Jie and Zhou and told Wang Wu of Lechang, "If our ruler paused here and asked what those two did wrong, he might learn something."

sentences.chinese: 畫人有堯、舜、禹、湯，不稱而舉桀、紂。
sentences.literal: The hall held portraits of Yao, Shun, Yu, and Tang, yet he named not them but Jie and Zhou.
sentences.idiomatic: The wall also showed Yao, Shun, Yu, and Tang—yet he singled out the tyrants.

sentences.chinese: 惲聞匈奴降者道單于見殺，惲曰：「得不肖君，大臣為畫善計不用，自令身無處所。
sentences.literal: When Yun heard from Xiongnu who had surrendered that the Chanyu had been killed, he said, "When you get a worthless ruler and his ministers lay good plans yet he will not use them, you end with nowhere to turn.
sentences.idiomatic: Hearing from surrendered nomads that their Chanyu had been murdered, Yun said, "Give a realm to a fool who ignores good counsel and even his ministers have nowhere to stand.

sentences.chinese: 若秦時但任小臣，誅殺忠良，竟以滅亡；
sentences.literal: It was like Qin times, when they trusted only petty officials, slaughtered the loyal and good, and in the end perished;
sentences.idiomatic: Qin fell because it trusted petty men and butchered its loyalists;

sentences.chinese: 令親任大臣，即至今耳。
sentences.literal: had they instead entrusted great ministers, they would still be ruling today.
sentences.idiomatic: had it trusted great ministers it might still reign today.

sentences.chinese: 古與今如一丘之貉。」
sentences.literal: "Ancient and modern are badgers from the same hill."
sentences.idiomatic: "Past and present are badgers dug from the same burrow."

sentences.chinese: 惲妄引亡國以誹謗當世，無人臣禮。
sentences.literal: Yun recklessly cited fallen states to slander the present age and showed no deportment befitting a subject.
sentences.idiomatic: He had invoked ruined dynasties to mock his own sovereign—conduct no subject may offer.

sentences.chinese: 又語長樂曰：「正月以來，天陰不雨，此《春秋》所記，夏侯君所言。
sentences.literal: He also told Changle, "Since the first month the sky has been overcast without rain—what the Spring and Autumn records, what Lord Xiahou spoke of.
sentences.idiomatic: He also told Dai, "Since new year the sky has stayed black without rain—the very sign the Spring and Autumn and Lord Xiahou warned against.

sentences.chinese: 行必不至河東矣。」
sentences.literal: "The journey surely will not reach Hedong."
sentences.idiomatic: "The imperial train will never reach Hedong."

sentences.chinese: 以主上為戲語，尤悖逆絕理。
sentences.literal: To treat the sovereign as the butt of jest was supremely perverse and defied all principle.
sentences.idiomatic: Jesting about the Son of Heaven crossed every line of loyalty.

sentences.chinese: 事下廷尉。
sentences.literal: The case was sent to the Commandant of Justice.
sentences.idiomatic: The file went to the commandant of justice.

sentences.chinese: 廷尉定國考問，左驗明白，奏：
sentences.literal: Commandant of Justice Dinggu examined him; corroboration on the left was clear, and he memorialized:
sentences.idiomatic: Commandant Yu Dinggu questioned witnesses until the evidence lay clear, then reported:

sentences.chinese: 惲不服罪，而召戶將尊，欲令戒飭富平侯延壽，曰：「太僕定有死罪數事，朝暮人也。
sentences.literal: Yun refused to confess guilt but summoned the household commandant Zun, wishing to have him warn Marquis of Fuping Yanshou, saying, "The Grand Coachman certainly has several capital crimes; he is a man who counts his days by morning and evening.
sentences.idiomatic: Yun refused to plead guilty but called in steward Zun to coach Zhang Yanshou: "The grand coachman sits on multiple capital offenses—he is living on borrowed time.

sentences.chinese: 惲幸與富平侯婚姻，今獨三人坐語，侯言『時不聞惲語』，自與太僕相觸也。」
sentences.literal: "I am fortunate to be affined to Marquis of Fuping; now the three of us sat talking alone; if the marquis says 'he did not hear Yun's words at the time,' he clashes directly with the Grand Coachman."
sentences.idiomatic: I am tied to Fuping by marriage; if the marquis later swears he never heard me speak, he perjures himself against Dai."

sentences.chinese: 尊曰：「不可。」
sentences.literal: Zun said, "It cannot be done."
sentences.idiomatic: Zun refused.

sentences.chinese: 惲怒，持大刀，曰：「蒙富平侯力，得族罪！
sentences.literal: Yun in anger seized a long knife and said, "Relying on Marquis of Fuping's pull I escaped clan execution!"
sentences.idiomatic: Yun drew a long knife and snarled, "Only Fuping's favor kept my clan alive!"

sentences.chinese: 毋洩惲語，令太僕聞之亂餘事。」
sentences.literal: "Do not leak what Yun said—let the Grand Coachman hear and he will confuse the rest of the case."
sentences.idiomatic: "Keep your mouth shut or Dai will wreck the rest of our story."

sentences.chinese: 惲幸得列九卿諸吏，宿衛近臣，上所信任，與聞政事，不竭忠愛，盡臣子義，而妄怨望，稱引為訞惡言，大逆不道，請逮捕治。
sentences.literal: Though Yun had ranked among the nine ministers and superintendents, a night guard and trusted confidant of the sovereign who heard state affairs, he did not exhaust loyal devotion or fulfill a son-of-the-soil's duty, but groundlessly nursed resentment and spouted malignant slander—it was great treason and impiety; let him be arrested and tried.
sentences.idiomatic: Though Yun had stood among the nine ministers and palace superintendents, heard every state secret, and enjoyed imperial trust, he had not repaid that trust with loyalty but spread venomous slander—conduct that amounted to great treason; Dinggu asked leave to clap him in chains.

sentences.chinese: 上不忍加誅，有詔皆免惲、長樂為庶人。
sentences.literal: The emperor could not bear to execute them; an edict degraded Yun and Changle alike to commoners.
sentences.idiomatic: Xuandi shrank from blood and issued an edict stripping both Yun and Dai to commoner status.

sentences.chinese: 惲既失爵位，家居治產業，起室宅，以財自娛。
sentences.literal: Once Yun had lost rank and title, he dwelt at home, managed his estate, raised house and hall, and took his pleasure in wealth.
sentences.idiomatic: Stripped of rank, Yun retired to his lands, built fine halls, and amused himself with his fortune.
